omission_flags: The analysis does not specify the political administrations involved during the 15-year saga (BN, PH, PN, and the current Unity Government), which might leave readers without context on who presided over specific escalations.

cardinal_sin_flags.why: The analysis confuses total project duration with delay. The first ship (KD Maharaja Lela) was originally scheduled for delivery in April 2019. A December 2026 delivery makes it 7.5 years late, not 15. The 15-year figure refers to the time elapsed since the Letter of Award (2011).
cardinal_sin_flags.fix: first ship delivered 15 years after the project's inception (7.5 years behind schedule)

cardinal_sin_flags.why: The budget rose from RM6 billion (initial estimate) to RM11.22 billion. This is a 1.87x increase. While close to double, 'twice' is a rounded-up exaggeration for editorial impact.

verification_log.claim: LCS cost has reached RM11.22 billion
verification_log.primary_source: Ministry of Defence statement, May 2023 (approved ceiling for 5 ships).

verification_log.claim: Maharaja Lela began sea trials on 28 January 2026
verification_log.primary_source: Bernama and Malay Mail reports, Jan 28, 2026 (Maiden sea-going trial).

verification_log.claim: Programme was reduced from six ships to five
verification_log.primary_source: Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan, May 2023.

verification_log.claim: Original 2011 budget was RM6 billion; the ceiling escalated to RM9 billion late that year
verification_log.primary_source: Free Malaysia Today (2022) citing initial estimates; LOA issued Dec 16, 2011 for RM9.13b.

verification_log.claim: MOF Inc acquired and renamed it Lumut Naval Shipyard in 2024
Royal Malaysian Navy has spent more than a decade waiting for vessels Singapore and Indonesia commissioned years ago
verification_log.primary_source: RSN Independence-class (2017) and TNI-AL SIGMA-class (2017) commissioning dates.

overall_assessment: The analysis is factually accurate regarding recent milestones, including the specific date of sea trials and the finalized budget ceiling. However, it significantly overclaims the duration of the delay by stating the ship is '15 years late' when it is actually 7.5 years late relative to its original due date (confusing project age with delay length). The tone is critical but aligns with the findings of the Public Accounts Committee (PAC). It successfully avoids partisan blame by framing the issue as a systemic procurement failure.
